messages.content: edit: In addition, we tested PLS marker variable modeling approach. We analyzed the model without marker variable design and with marker variable and compared the two steps, there were a slightly differences in terms of path coefficients. Also, the paths of marker variable to the endogenous constructs were not significant.

Furthermore, we conducted testing on the PLS marker variable modeling approach. We evaluated the model both with and without a marker variable design, and compared the results between the two approaches. There were some minor differences observed in the path coefficients between the two steps. Additionally, the paths of the marker variable to the endogenous constructs did not show any significant results.

this discussion is correct? Furthermore, we conducted testing on the PLS marker variable approach. We evaluated the model both with and without a marker variable design, and compared the results between the two steps. There were only slightly differences observed in the path coefficients between the two steps. Additionally, the paths of the marker variable to the endogenous constructs did not show any significant results. Thus, the test confirmed the absence of significant systematic common method bias in the data.

Yes, the discussion is correct. The testing on the PLS marker variable approach involved evaluating the model with and without a marker variable design, and comparing the results between the two steps. The minor differences observed in the path coefficients between the two steps indicate that there was no significant systematic common method bias in the data. The lack of significant results in the paths of the marker variable to the endogenous constructs further confirms this. Therefore, the test conducted in this study supports the conclusion that the data was not affected by significant common method bias.

edit: The study recruited IT managers as a single respondent. This could lead to potential common methods bias (Newkirk et al., 2003). Common method bias was assessed using exploratory factor analysis (EFA). The EFA revealed that eight items had an eigenvalue greater than one and none were greater than the variance (i.e., variances ranged from 2.922% to 41.376%). Thus, the test confirmed the absence of significant systematic common method bias in the data. Furthermore, we conducted testing on the PLS marker variable approach(Rönkkö & Ylitalo, 2011). We evaluated the model both with and without a marker variable design, and compared the results between the two steps. There were only slightly differences observed in the path coefficients between the two steps. Additionally, the paths of the marker variable to the endogenous constructs did not show any significant results (p values from 0.477 to 0.910) Thus, data was not affected by significant common method bias.

The study was conducted with IT managers as single respondents, which may have caused potential common methods bias, as noted by Newkirk et al. (2003). To assess common method bias, an exploratory factor analysis (EFA) was performed. The EFA results showed that none of the eight items had an eigenvalue greater than the variance, which ranged from 2.922% to 41.376%. Therefore, the absence of significant systematic common method bias in the data was confirmed. Additionally, we conducted a test of the PLS marker variable approach (Rönkkö & Ylitalo, 2011) by evaluating the model with and without a marker variable design and comparing the results between the two steps. There were only minor differences observed in the path coefficients between the two steps, and the paths of the marker variable to the endogenous constructs did not show any significant results (with p values ranging from 0.477 to 0.910). Thus, our test results support the conclusion that the data was not affected by significant common method bias.
a litle sumraize?

The study recruited IT managers as respondents, which may have led to common method bias. However, to test for this bias, an exploratory factor analysis was performed, which confirmed the absence of significant systematic common method bias in the data. Furthermore, the PLS marker variable approach was tested by comparing the model with and without a marker variable design, and only minor differences were observed in the path coefficients. The paths of the marker variable to the endogenous constructs did not show any significant results, further supporting the lack of significant common method bias in the data.

edit: Common method bias was assessed using exploratory factor analysis (EFA). The EFA revealed that eight items had an eigenvalue greater than one and none were greater than the variance (i.e., variances ranged from 2.922% to 41.376%) (Newkirk et al., 2003). Furthermore, we conducted testing on the PLS marker variable approach(Rönkkö & Ylitalo, 2011). We evaluated the model both with and without a marker variable design, and compared the results between the two steps. There were only slightly differences observed in the path coefficients between the two steps. Additionally, the paths of the marker variable to the endogenous constructs did not show any significant results (p values from 0.477 to 0.910) Thus, the test confirmed the absence of significant systematic common method bias in the data.

To test for common method bias, an exploratory factor analysis was conducted, which showed that the eight items had an eigenvalue greater than one and none were greater than the variance, ranging from 2.922% to 41.376% (Newkirk et al., 2003). Additionally, a test was performed on the PLS marker variable approach, comparing the model with and without a marker variable design. The results showed only minor differences in path coefficients, with no significant results observed in the paths of the marker variable to the endogenous constructs (p-values ranging from 0.477 to 0.910). These findings support the conclusion that there was no significant systematic common method bias in the data.
again?

Sure, here is a revised version of the summary:

The study examined the potential for common method bias by analyzing the data with an exploratory factor analysis. This analysis showed that none of the items had an eigenvalue greater than the variance, and eight items had an eigenvalue greater than one, indicating that there was no significant systematic common method bias in the data. Further testing of the PLS marker variable approach was conducted, which involved comparing the model both with and without a marker variable design. The results showed only minor differences in path coefficients, and the paths of the marker variable to the endogenous constructs did not produce significant results. These findings further support the conclusion that there was no significant common method bias in the data.
again pleaes/

Certainly, here is a further revised version of the summary:

The study took measures to address the potential for common method bias. One method was the exploratory factor analysis, which showed that none of the items had an eigenvalue greater than the variance and eight items had an eigenvalue greater than one, which suggested that there was no significant systematic common method bias in the data. Additionally, the PLS marker variable approach was employed to reduce the potential for common method bias. This approach involved testing both the model with and without a marker variable design and comparing the results. The results of this test showed only minor differences in path coefficients and no significant results were observed in the paths of the marker variable to the endogenous constructs, confirming the absence of significant common method bias in the data.
edit: These findings support the conclusion that there was no significant systematic common method bias in the data.

The results of both the exploratory factor analysis and the PLS marker variable approach suggest that there was no significant systematic common method bias in the data. Therefore, this study's findings support the conclusion that the data was not significantly affected by common method bias.
sumarize?

In summary, to assess potential common method bias, this study conducted an exploratory factor analysis and tested the PLS marker variable approach. The exploratory factor analysis showed that there was no significant systematic common method bias in the data, and the PLS marker variable approach confirmed these findings by showing only minor differences in path coefficients and no significant results in the paths of the marker variable to the endogenous constructs. The overall results support the conclusion that the data was not significantly affected by common method bias.
edit only  this sentence: These findings support the conclusion that there was no significant systematic common method bias in the data.
The results of both the exploratory factor analysis and the PLS marker variable approach suggest that the data was not significantly affected by common method bias.

edit: Additionally, a test was performed on the PLS marker variable approach, comparing the model with and without a marker variable design (Rönkkö & Ylitalo, 2011). The results showed that there is only a slight difference in the path coefficients of the model with a marker variable compared to the model without the marker variable. Also, no significant results observed in the paths of the marker variable to the endogenous constructs (p-values ranging from 0.477 to 0.910).

The researchers further tested the potential for common method bias by utilizing the PLS marker variable approach and comparing the model with and without a marker variable design (Rönkkö & Ylitalo, 2011). The findings indicate that there were only minor differences observed in the path coefficients of the model with a marker variable compared to the model without the marker variable. Additionally, there were no significant results observed in the paths of the marker variable to the endogenous constructs, with p-values ranging from 0.477 to 0.910, implying that there was no significant common method bias in the data.

Sure, here's another potential revision:

To address the possibility of common method bias, the researchers implemented the PLS marker variable approach and compared the model with and without a marker variable design (Rönkkö & Ylitalo, 2011). The analysis revealed that the model with the marker variable showed only slight differences in path coefficients when compared to the model without the variable. Furthermore, the paths of the marker variable to the endogenous constructs were not significant, with p-values ranging from 0.477 to 0.910, implying the absence of significant common method bias in the data.

Analysis of the PLS marker variable approach revealed that the inclusion of a marker variable in the model resulted in only minimal differences in path coefficients when compared to the model without the variable.
